intro: Porting TypeScript to Ruby transitions a codebase into a highly expressive, purely object-oriented dynamic scripting language optimized for developer happiness and rapid application prototyping. Developers make this switch when adopting frameworks like Ruby on Rails to build robust, database-driven web applications with minimal boilerplate. This shift requires moving from explicit compile-time type verification to dynamic runtime method evaluation and duck-typing mechanics.

diffs.from: Strict structural static type layer verified during compilation and erased before execution.
diffs.to: Strongly typed dynamic system centered around duck-typing, where object capabilities are verified at runtime when methods are called.

diffs.from: C-style block structures requiring curly braces, semicolons, and explicit return markers.
diffs.to: Clean, human-readable layout omitting curly braces and semicolons, utilizing trailing blocks and implicit expression returns.

diffs.from: Managed automatically via engine generational garbage collection sweeps on a persistent application heap.
diffs.to: Automated object slot reference tracking combined with a generational mark-and-sweep garbage collector.

diffs.from: Single-threaded event loop architecture executing non-blocking Promise queues natively.
diffs.to: Synchronous by default, utilizing Fiber objects, concurrent thread abstractions, or Guild isolation patterns for parallel logic.

diffs.from: Minimal core focusing on JavaScript foundations, relying heavily on npm packages for expanded features.
diffs.to: Extensive standard library offering powerful string manipulators, file handling utilities, and native system scripting tools.

examples.title: Class and Initialization Blocks
examples.description: Declaring regular classes with instance variable setters.

examples.title: Block-Based Collection Transformations
examples.fromCode:
const inputs: number[] = [1, 2, 3, 4];
const odds: number[] = inputs.filter(x => x % 2 !== 0);

tips.title: Leverage Implicit Return Values
tips.body: Ruby functions automatically return the value of the last evaluated expression inside their block scope. You can safely omit the `return` keyword unless you need to exit a function early based on a conditional check.

tips.title: Understand Everything is an Object
tips.body: Unlike TypeScript, Ruby contains no primitive values or standalone functions. Every entity—including numbers, nil states, and class definitions—is a true object that inherits from the base Object class.

tips.title: Replace Callbacks with Blocks
tips.body: Instead of passing anonymous arrow functions as callbacks, use Ruby's native blocks passed via curly braces or `do...end` markers, which are received using the internal `yield` keyword.

tips.title: Verify Interface Contracts via RBS
tips.body: If you miss TypeScript's compile-time validation, adopt Ruby's modern typing companion files (`.rbs`) alongside static analysis engines like Steep to check parameter constraints before execution.
